A mantis shrimp punches so fast it boils the water around its claw, creating a flash of light called sonoluminescence.
Tardigrades can survive being frozen, boiled, dried out, and even exposed to the vacuum of space.
An octopus has three hearts and blue blood, and two of those hearts stop beating when it swims.
Immortal jellyfish can revert their cells to an earlier stage and start their life cycle over, seemingly forever.
A hummingbird's heart can beat over 1,200 times a minute, and it may visit over a thousand flowers in a day.
Sloths move so slowly that algae grows on their fur, giving them a greenish camouflage coat.
Some sharks glow in the dark, using light-producing organs to blend into the faint glow from the surface above.
Elephants can recognize themselves in a mirror, a rare sign of self-awareness shared with only a few species.
A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance, and their pink color comes entirely from the food they eat.
Crows can hold grudges against specific humans and teach their offspring to recognize that person's face.
Wombats produce cube-shaped poop, the only known animal to make dung with flat sides.
Peregrine falcons dive at over 240 miles per hour, making them the fastest animals on the planet.
A cheetah can go from zero to sixty miles per hour in about three seconds, faster than most sports cars.
Axolotls can regrow entire limbs, parts of their heart, and even portions of their brain.
Honeybees communicate the location of food by performing a figure-eight waggle dance.
A shrimp's heart is located in its head, tucked just behind its eyes.
Pistol shrimp snap their claws to create a bubble that hits nearly the temperature of the sun's surface.
Arctic terns migrate from pole to pole each year, flying a round trip of about 44,000 miles.
Owls cannot move their eyeballs, so they rotate their heads up to 270 degrees to look around.
Naked mole rats feel almost no pain from acid or capsaicin and rarely develop cancer.
Some frogs freeze solid over winter, stopping their heart entirely, then thaw and hop away in spring.
Dolphins have names for each other, using signature whistles they invent as calves.
A woodpecker's tongue wraps around the back of its skull, cushioning its brain against the pounding.
Kangaroos cannot walk backward, which is part of why one appears on Australia's coat of arms.
Electric eels can deliver a shock of 600 volts, enough to stun a horse.
Cuttlefish can change both the color and the texture of their skin in a fraction of a second.
A single colony of leafcutter ants can move more soil than an earthworm and farm their own fungus for food.
Reindeer eyes change color between summer and winter, shifting from gold to blue to see in low light.
Bats are the only mammals capable of true sustained flight.
A group of ravens is fittingly called an unkindness or a conspiracy.
Sea otters hold hands while sleeping so they don't drift apart on the water.
Male seahorses carry and give birth to the babies, not the females.
Some geckos can run across water, using rapid steps and surface tension to stay on top.
Pigeons can be trained to distinguish paintings by Monet from those by Picasso.
A hippo's sweat is red and works as both sunscreen and an antibiotic.
Chameleons don't change color mainly for camouflage; they do it to communicate mood and regulate temperature.
The pistol-like snap of a mantis shrimp can shatter aquarium glass.
Alpine swifts can stay airborne for over six months without ever landing.
A tiger's skin is striped, not just its fur, and no two tigers share the same pattern.
Cows have best friends and become stressed when they are separated from them.
Vampire bats will share regurgitated blood with hungry roost-mates who fed them before.
A snail can sleep for up to three years when conditions are too dry.
Certain beetles can pull over 1,000 times their own body weight.
Giraffes only need about 30 minutes of sleep a day, often taken in short naps.
The mimic octopus can impersonate lionfish, sea snakes, and flatfish to fool predators.
A group of jellyfish is called a smack, and some smacks stretch for miles across the ocean.
Male emperor penguins balance an egg on their feet through the Antarctic winter without eating for months.
Ants never sleep in the way we do, but some rest in short bursts hundreds of times a day.
A star-nosed mole can identify and eat prey in under a quarter of a second, the fastest known forager.
Koalas have fingerprints so similar to ours they could confuse a crime scene.
Bombardier beetles spray a boiling, chemical jet from their rear to repel attackers.
The tongue of a blue whale alone can weigh around 6,000 pounds.
Some spiders balloon through the air by releasing silk that catches electric fields in the atmosphere.
A hummingbird is the only bird that can fly backward.
Emperor dragonflies catch prey in mid-air with a success rate above 95 percent.
Certain turtles can breathe through their rear ends while hibernating underwater.
A jaguar's bite is strong enough to crack a turtle's shell and pierce a skull.
Bald eagles build the largest tree nests of any animal, some weighing over a ton.
The lyrebird can perfectly mimic chainsaws, camera shutters, and car alarms.
A group of porcupines is called a prickle.
Camels can drink up to 30 gallons of water in about 13 minutes.
Male anglerfish fuse permanently onto the much larger female, becoming little more than a source of sperm.
Some ants explode themselves, rupturing their bodies to coat enemies in sticky toxic goo.
A goldfish can remember things for months, not the three seconds of the myth.
Platypuses have no stomach and hunt using electric fields with their bills, closing their eyes underwater.
The heart of a hibernating groundhog can slow to just four or five beats per minute.
Sperm whales sleep vertically, floating upright in the water like giant bobbing logs.
A python can go over a year without eating after a single large meal.
Fleas can jump around 100 times their own body length.
Some cicadas stay underground for 17 years before emerging all at once to breed.
The wood frog's blood contains natural antifreeze that protects it while frozen solid.
A jellyfish is about 95 percent water and has no brain, heart, or bones.
Sea cucumbers can eject their internal organs to distract predators, then grow them back.
Male peacock spiders dance and flash bright flaps of color to court females.
A rhinoceros horn is made of keratin, the same protein as human hair and fingernails.
Ostriches can run at 45 miles per hour and their eyes are bigger than their brains.
The archerfish shoots down insects by spitting a precise jet of water from below the surface.
A hagfish can produce enough slime in seconds to clog the gills of an attacking shark.
Herring communicate by releasing bubbles from their rear ends, effectively talking through farts.
Some parrots can live over 80 years, outliving their owners.
A crocodile's jaw can bite down with immense force but the muscles to open it are surprisingly weak.
Elephants can hear each other over miles using low rumbles felt through the ground with their feet.
The bar-tailed godwit can fly over 7,000 miles nonstop, without pausing to eat or drink.
Salmon can leap up waterfalls several feet high on their journey upstream to spawn.
A tarantula can survive months without food and regrow lost legs when it molts.
Some moths drink the tears of sleeping birds for salt and moisture.
The male bowerbird builds and decorates elaborate structures to impress a mate.
Manta rays have the largest brains of any fish and appear to recognize themselves in mirrors.
A leech has 32 brains, or more precisely 32 segments each with its own nerve cluster.
Nine-banded armadillos almost always give birth to identical quadruplets.
Certain frogs are so transparent you can watch their hearts beat through their skin.
A snapping turtle's ancestors swam alongside the dinosaurs and have barely changed since.
Termite mounds have built-in ventilation systems that keep the interior at a steady temperature.
Male fireflies flash in species-specific patterns, and some females mimic other species to lure and eat them.
A giant Pacific octopus can squeeze its whole body through a gap the size of a coin.
Narwhals have a single spiral tusk that is actually an overgrown tooth packed with nerve endings.
Some deep-sea fish make their own light using glowing bacteria living inside their bodies.
A flea's jump accelerates faster than a space shuttle at launch.
Prairie dogs have one of the most complex animal languages, with calls describing a predator's size and color.
Certain wasps lay eggs inside caterpillars, and the larvae eat the host from the inside out.
The tuatara of New Zealand has a light-sensitive third eye on the top of its head.
A pistol shrimp and a goby fish often share a burrow, trading protection for digging.
Male mosquitoes don't bite; only the females need blood to develop their eggs.
Spinner dolphins can leap from the water and spin up to seven times in a single jump.
A hedgehog has around 5,000 spines, each of which lasts about a year before falling out.
The colossal squid has eyes the size of dinner plates, the largest of any animal.
Some geese fly at altitudes over 20,000 feet to cross the Himalayas during migration.
A group of crows is called a murder, a name dating back to old folklore about the birds.
